title: Climate Grief and Climate Rage: A Working Distinction
dek: Climate grief and rage manifest profoundly in the body, revealing a deep somatic dialogue with the ongoing environmental crisis. The tension, tightness, and aches felt in muscles and breath reflect not just emotional responses but biological reverberations of loss and anger. Drawing on insights from trauma and mindfulness experts, this article explores how these physical sensations serve as urgent messengers, urging awareness and compassionate action in the face of climate change.
bodyMarkdown:
## Introduction

Your jaw clenches first. The masseter muscle, thick and unyielding, knots beneath your skin like a clenched fist made flesh. It’s early evening, the air is dense with heat and the faint sting of wildfire smoke—an acrid reminder of the burning forests you saw on the news. That smell seeps into your sinuses, scratches at the lining of your throat, thickens the wetness of your breath. There’s a tightening around your sternum, almost like your diaphragm is trying to shrink away from the world’s suffocating reality. This is not just sadness or anger; this is the body speaking in its own ancient tongue—one the mind struggles to translate.

Climate grief lives here, in the hollow just beneath your breastbone, where your heart beats a slow, heavy rhythm. It is a slow constriction, the levator scapulae at the nape of your neck stiffening as if bracing against an invisible storm. You feel the wetness of tears that come unbidden, not from weakness but from the overwhelming sense of loss—the forests that once stretched for miles now reduced to ash, the waterways running hotter, stranger, unrecognizable. The grief is a deep, aching pressure—a fascia-bound lament wrapped tight around your ribs, an unspoken elegy for what the earth was and what it can no longer be.

But climate rage—ah, that is a different beast. It rises hot, sudden, like a flare lighting the trapezius muscles that snap tight across your shoulders. Your breath shortens, the air feels thick and contaminated, making each inhale a small battle. Rage pulses in the veins behind your eyes, the temple muscles throb with electric sharpness. It is a fierce, urgent energy that demands action, that shakes your core like a thunderclap in the night. The body remembers this too—Robert Augustus Masters speaks of how rage can be a gateway, a primal force that, when channeled, can ignite profound change. But untreated, it can calcify, turning your muscles into armor so rigid you forget you ever meant to soften.

The air quality matters here, not just metaphorically but biologically. When the air is heavy with particulate matter, your lungs react with a rawness that mirrors your inner turmoil. You can taste the smoke on your tongue, feel the grit settling deep in your bronchial tubes. The body knows this is not just pollution outside but a tempest within. Thich Nhat Hanh reminds us that the body is a home; it’s also a frontline. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body’s rage and grief are not failures—they are messengers, urgent and unvarnished.

You might reach for herbs like chamomile or passionflower, their bitter-sweetness a balm to the tight diaphragm and racing heart, or perhaps you clutch a cup of dried skullcap, its nervine properties coaxing your system toward release. These are not magic fixes but allies in a somatic conversation you are only just learning to hear. Resmaa Menakem teaches that trauma lives in the body’s muscles and fascia, and climate grief and rage are no different—they are the earth’s trauma echoing in your tissues.

Tonight, when you feel that familiar tightness, that fierce pulse, remember this: your body remembers. The jaw remembers. The chest remembers. The air you breathe remembers. And through this remembering, you are invited into a fierce, sacred dialogue with the world’s breaking heart.

## The Biology of Grief

The body carries grief like a dark thread woven through muscle and breath, long before the mind can name the loss. When you think about the collapse of forests or the vanishing of wetlands, it’s not just an intellectual sorrow. Your psoas tightens, that deep core muscle anchoring your spine and hips, the very seat of your fight-or-flight readiness. It contracts as if bracing you against a storm you cannot outrun. This is not metaphor. The psoas is wired to register threat and loss, and it remembers the silence of species gone, the absence of green where there was once abundance.

At night, when the world is still and the diaphragm — that central muscle of breath — slows its rhythm, the weight of sorrow presses down like a physical thing. It nestles between ribs and stomach, a sinking heaviness you can smell in the stale, recycled air of a city that no longer feels alive. You might find your breath shallow, clipped, the chest tight as if the body itself is holding back tears it cannot release. Pema Chödrön, who spent decades sitting with suffering, reminds us that the body is the first to know grief. She teaches that the breath is where grief lives before words arrive, and that opening the diaphragm gently, like coaxing a fragile bird from its cage, is a way to start moving through that dark.

Francis Weller, whose work with grief is rooted deeply in ceremony and somatic awareness, talks about how grief is a communal body memory. It’s not just your loss, it’s ancestral and planetary, a deep mourning that pulses through the fascia and connective tissue of the collective body. When ecosystems perish, it is as if the earth’s own psoas seizes in pain, the diaphragm struggles to draw in clean air, and the body’s sorrow compounds, becoming a weight that anchors you in place, or pushes you into a restless rage.

This is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows the loss before the mind can articulate it. The tightness in your trapezius, the dull ache in your levator scapulae, these are not incidental but integral parts of processing what it means to watch the world unravel. To honor this, you might work with herbs that support the nervous system — skullcap or California poppy, taken as a tincture in the late evening when the body’s grief surfaces most palpably. These are medicines that recognize the body’s wisdom, not trying to erase the sorrow but to hold it with gentleness, so the psoas can begin to release, the diaphragm can open, and the breath can carry the weight of loss without breaking.

Grief is not an abstract concept; it is a visceral, embodied experience that demands your full attention. When you listen to your body’s mourning, you begin to distinguish the quiet sorrow of grief from the sharp, burning energy of rage. Both are messengers. Both are necessary. But the body, with its muscles and fascia, its breath and its herbs, is where you start to make that working distinction real.

## The Biology of Rage

You’re sitting at your desk, the late afternoon sun cutting through the window just so, warming the back of your neck—and suddenly your jaw clenches. It’s subtle at first, a tightening that presses the masseter muscle along your cheekbone and temple. You can feel the fascia gripping beneath your fingers, like a cord pulled taut. This is the biology of rage, the heat of climate anger. It isn’t some abstract feeling. It is a physical fact: the body’s way of trying to get your attention, of demanding movement when the world feels stuck in its own slow burn.

Karla McLaren, who has taught me so much about the languages of anger, reminds us that rage is a messenger with very precise instructions. It doesn’t want you to stew or ruminate; it wants you to move. The trapezius muscle—those wide wings spanning your shoulders and neck—pulls tight like a bowstring wound for release. In moments when the news of glaciers melting or fires racing through ancient forests floods your awareness, you might catch that trapezius tensing. Your body is preparing for action, even if your mind feels defeated.

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic trauma therapist, talks about how this tension doesn’t just sit in your head or heart but lodges deep in the muscles around your neck and shoulders. He calls it “racialized trauma,” but his work carries across to all forms of collective stress, including the grief and rage that rise from witnessing the earth’s suffering. When your levator scapulae—the small muscles lifting your shoulder blades—lock up, it’s not just discomfort. It’s the body’s way of bracing against an invisible threat. Your nervous system is on notice, alert to the danger it senses in the news, the conversations, the endless stream of images.

And yet, the body doesn’t want you to stay frozen in this tension. That urgent heat in your psoas, the deep core muscle that anchors your spine and hips, is a call to move, to reclaim agency. This is why so often, climate rage manifests as restless pacing, clenching fists, or sudden bursts of need to run or shout. The anger is alive there, in the diaphragm’s quickening breaths and the way your ribs expand and contract under your fingers. It is not pathology. It is biology.

The jaw remembers what your mind might try to forget. The trapezius speaks a language older than words. When you feel that heat rising, lean into the urging to shift—stretch your neck slowly, roll your shoulders, or step outside and let the earth beneath your feet absorb some of the fire. Herbs like skullcap or passionflower can ease the muscle tension, but the first medicine is always honoring the body’s call to move. This is how you hold climate rage without letting it consume you. This is how you listen to the body’s heat and respond with care.

## The False Dichotomy

**Q:** Why are we so often told to calm down about climate change, and why does the body refuse to cooperate?

**A:** You feel it in your masseter muscles first—the jaw clenches tight like a trap, the fascia around the temples taut as if holding back a scream that’s been building since you learned the planet was dying. Someone tells you, “Calm down,” and your body rebels. This isn’t a moral failing or a temper tantrum; this is biology. The insistence that you “calm down” about the climate crisis is a quiet but powerful tool wielded by the status quo. It’s a message that your body’s urgent signals—anger, grief, rage—are inconvenient, dangerous, and must be muted.

The suppression of anger, especially women’s anger, is as old as the colonial systems that exploit land and people alike. When you’re told to “stay calm,” it’s not about your well-being. It’s about maintaining control. It’s the same cultural prescription that told you your fury was ugly, unladylike, or disruptive. It’s a command designed to sever you from the raw, somatic truth that your body knows: this is a crisis demanding fire, not ice. The trapezius tightens, the levator scapulae knots, and the diaphragm contracts shallowly because your nervous system is trying to hold the line against a tidal wave of grief and rage that wants to break free.

Teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that trauma lives in the body, that the body remembers what the mind tries to forget. Your climate rage isn’t pathology; it’s your nervous system’s way of signaling that something is deeply, systemically wrong. When you shove that rage down, you’re not just silencing yourself—you’re participating in a collective anesthesia that keeps harmful systems intact. This is the same dynamic that Bessel van der Kolk describes in trauma survivors: suppressing the body’s natural impulses only deepens the wound.

Thich Nhat Hanh’s teaching on “embracing anger as a path to peace” resonates here. Your body’s refusal to calm down is a form of somatic wisdom. It’s the psoas muscle tightening because it’s preparing to fight for survival, not to sit quietly and accept the slow violence of ecological collapse. The anger is a messenger, not a monster. When you sip a cup of [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) in the late evening, letting the warmth settle into your solar plexus, you’re honoring that message. The body knows what needs to be done, but first, it demands to be heard.

This is why the false dichotomy of “calm grieving” versus “raging outburst” must be dismantled. Your body doesn’t want neat categories; it wants integration. It wants to move through the fire of rage and the ache of grief together, without shame, without apology. The suppression of your anger is not just a personal injunction—it’s a political act that maintains the status quo. When you reclaim your right to feel fully, you begin to crack open the possibility of real change.

## Herbal Support for the Overwhelmed Nervous System

When the weight of the world crushes your chest—when the news cycle feels like a relentless drumbeat against your ribs, and your diaphragm is caught in a spasm that won’t relent—herbs become a language your body understands before your mind can catch up. Climate grief isn’t just a concept; it is a lived, breathing contraction in the masseter muscle, a clenching of the levator scapulae at the base of your skull, a tightening of the psoas that feels like the earth itself has folded inward inside you. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows, and it’s screaming in muscle and fascia.

Milky Oats (Avena sativa) is one of those herbs that speaks gently to the nervous system’s frayed edges. When your limbs tremble with exhaustion from carrying the unbearable, Milky Oats seeps into the fascia around your nerves like a balm. It’s not a sedative that knocks you out; it’s a restorative, rich in minerals and alkaloids that support the adrenal glands, the very organs that keep firing when your nervous system is running hot on climate rage and despair. The oat straw’s silica-rich structure nourishes the nervous system’s connective tissue, the myelin sheath that wraps around your neurons like insulation, helping the messages from your brain to your body slow down and become less jagged. At 3 a.m., when your heart thrums against your ribs like an anxious drummer, a tincture of Milky Oats can smooth the jittery edges, offering a moment where the jaw loosens and the trapezius muscle unwinds from its iron grip.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is a different kind of medicine, something that settles into the body’s overactive electrical wiring. It works on the GABA receptors in the brain, the same ones that Thich Nhat Hanh described as the “peacekeepers” of the nervous system. When the news of melting glaciers and burning forests ignites a wildfire in your chest, Skullcap acts like a manual dimmer switch, lowering the intensity of that internal blaze. It doesn’t numb; it doesn’t deny your rage. Instead, it gives your diaphragm permission to soften, to expand fully again, and the tightness around your solar plexus—the place where so much anxiety sits—relaxes just enough for you to breathe through the despair without losing yourself to it. The smell of fresh Skullcap tincture, sharp and green, lingers in the air like a promise that the body’s electric storm can calm.

Hawthorn (Crataegus spp.) moves through the body differently, anchoring its medicine in the heart itself—literally the cardiac muscle. Climate grief often manifests as a heaviness in the chest, a constriction that’s not just metaphorical. Hawthorn’s flavonoids strengthen the heart’s contractions, improve circulation, and soothe the vagus nerve, the main highway between your brain and your gut. When you feel the weight of the world pressing down on your sternum, Hawthorn reminds the heart that it can hold the grief without breaking. It’s a medicine of resilience, a reminder that the body can endure the ache of loss while still pumping blood, still moving forward. You might take it as a strong infusion, the deep red liquid tasting of earth and survival, a somatic anchor in a world that feels unmoored.

These herbs are not quick fixes. They are allies in a slow, deliberate conversation with your body’s grief and rage. They don’t erase the pain; they give your nervous system the scaffolding it needs to hold it. When you reach for [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20) or brew a pot of Hawthorn tea, you are telling your body: I hear you. I feel the tightness in the psoas, the clench in the jaw. I am not turning away. This is the work of somatic herbalism—meeting the body where it is, in all its messy, biological truth, and offering it the medicine it needs to keep breathing.

## Somatic Practice

The body knows the difference between the heavy, sinking weight of climate grief and the hot, electric charge of climate rage. When the news cycle hits you like a physical blow, your masseter muscle tightens, your jaw clenches, and the fascia around your neck and shoulders pulls taut. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your nervous system is bracing for impact, and the energy of that rage needs somewhere to go. If you don’t move it, it settles into the psoas, the deep core muscle that connects your spine to your legs, turning your body into a rigid container for unexpressed fury.

To move this energy, you must start with the ground. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, feeling the solid earth beneath you. Notice the way your toes grip the floor, the tension in your calves. This is the foundation. Now, bring your attention to your hands. Let them hang loosely by your sides. Begin to shake them, gently at first, then with more vigor. Feel the vibration moving up your arms, into your shoulders, and down your spine. This is not a polite movement; it is a primal release. The body remembers how to shake off trauma, how to discharge the excess energy that builds up when we are forced to witness the destruction of our world.

As you shake, focus on your breath. Inhale deeply through your nose, feeling your diaphragm expand, pushing your belly out. Exhale forcefully through your mouth, making a sound if you need to—a sigh, a groan, a shout. Let the sound vibrate in your chest, in the tight spaces between your ribs. This is the sound of your rage, moving out of the fascia and into the air. Notice how the trapezius muscle, the one that runs from your neck down your back, begins to soften. The levator scapulae, the muscle that lifts your shoulder blades, releases its grip. The body is unwinding, layer by layer.

When you feel the energy begin to settle, stop shaking. Stand still and notice the sensations in your body. The tingling in your hands, the warmth in your chest, the steady rhythm of your heart. You have moved the rage through your muscles, honoring its presence without letting it consume you. This is the work of somatic practice—not to eliminate the anger, but to give it a channel, a way to move through the body so that you can remain present, grounded, and ready for whatever comes next.

## FAQ

**Q:** How do I know if what I’m feeling is climate grief or climate rage?

**A:** The body knows the difference, even if the mind is confused. Climate grief often feels like a heavy, sinking weight in the chest. Your diaphragm might feel tight, making it hard to take a deep breath. The psoas muscle, deep in your core, might ache with a dull, persistent tension. It’s a feeling of collapse, of being pulled downward by the sheer magnitude of the loss. Climate rage, on the other hand, is hot and electric. It lives in the masseter muscle of your jaw, the trapezius across your shoulders, and the levator scapulae at the back of your neck. It’s a sharp, urgent energy that makes you want to move, to shout, to fight back. Both are valid, biological responses to a world in crisis.

**Q:** Why does my body react so strongly to news about climate change?

**A:** This is not pathology, this is biology. Your nervous system is designed to respond to threats, and the destruction of our environment is a profound threat to our survival. When you read about wildfires or melting ice caps, your body doesn’t distinguish between a distant crisis and an immediate danger. The fight-or-flight response is triggered, flooding your system with adrenaline and cortisol. The fascia tightens, the muscles brace, and the breath becomes shallow. Your body is doing exactly what it evolved to do—preparing you to survive.

**Q:** Can herbs really help with these intense emotions?

**A:** Yes, but they are not magic bullets. Herbs are allies that support the body’s natural ability to regulate itself. When your nervous system is overwhelmed by climate rage, nervines like Skullcap or Milky Oats can help soothe the frayed edges, allowing the diaphragm to relax and the breath to deepen. When grief sits heavy in the chest, Hawthorn can support the physical and emotional heart, reminding the body that it can hold the sorrow without breaking. These medicines work with the body, not against it, honoring the biological reality of your emotions.

**Q:** What should I do when the rage feels like too much to handle?

**A:** Move it. The body needs to discharge the energy of rage, or it will settle into the muscles and fascia, causing chronic tension and pain. Shake your hands, stomp your feet, or scream into a pillow. Let the energy move through your trapezius, your psoas, your masseter. Honor the heat of the anger, and give it a physical outlet. This is not about losing control; it’s about giving the body what it needs to process the intensity of the emotion.

## Deeper into the Body

The distinction between climate grief and climate rage is not just a matter of emotional tone; it is a profound somatic reality, etched into the very architecture of your muscles and fascia. When you wake at 3 a.m., the sheets damp with sweat, the air thick with the distant smell of smoke, your body is already deep in conversation with the burning world. The mind might still be groggy, fumbling for context, but the body knows. The body always knows.

Climate grief is a slow, heavy descent. It settles into the psoas, that deep, primal muscle that connects your lumbar spine to your legs. The psoas is the muscle of the fetal position, the muscle that curls you inward when the world is too much to bear. When you are carrying the weight of ecological collapse—the vanishing of species, the poisoning of rivers—the psoas tightens, pulling your center of gravity down. You might feel a dull ache in your lower back, a sense of being anchored to the earth by a sorrow so dense it feels like lead. The diaphragm, the great dome of muscle that orchestrates your breath, becomes sluggish. Your inhales are shallow, your exhales long and ragged. The body is mourning, and it does so by slowing down, by contracting, by holding the grief in the dark, quiet spaces of the fascia.

But climate rage is a different animal entirely. It does not curl inward; it explodes outward. It is the heat that rises in the back of your neck, the sudden, sharp tension in the levator scapulae that lifts your shoulders toward your ears as if preparing for a blow. The masseter muscle in your jaw locks down, the teeth grinding together with a force that can crack enamel. This is the biology of fight, the nervous system’s desperate attempt to mobilize against a threat that feels both omnipresent and untouchable. The rage pulses in the forearms, a restless, electric energy that demands movement. It is the body screaming, *Do something, anything, to stop this.*

This is where the false dichotomy of "calm down" becomes not just dismissive, but biologically harmful. When you are told to suppress this rage, to be "reasonable" or "peaceful," you are being asked to wage war against your own nervous system. The energy of that rage does not simply vanish; it gets trapped in the tissues. The trapezius becomes a knot of chronic pain, the jaw develops TMJ, the breath remains trapped in the upper chest. The suppression of anger is a violence against the body, a denial of the very mechanisms that evolved to keep you alive and fighting for your habitat.

Herbal medicine offers a way to support the body through this intense somatic landscape, not by numbing the feelings, but by giving the nervous system the resilience it needs to process them. When the psoas is tight with grief, a strong infusion of [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) can offer profound support. Hawthorn is a medicine for the heart, both physical and emotional. It helps to open the chest, to remind the cardiovascular system that it can hold the weight of sorrow without collapsing. It is a grounding, stabilizing force that allows the grief to move through the body rather than getting stuck in the core.

For the hot, electric charge of climate rage, nervines like Skullcap and Milky Oats are essential allies. Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) works directly on the nervous system, helping to cool the heat of anger and release the tension in the masseter and trapezius. It does not sedate the rage; it simply takes the edge off, allowing the body to discharge the energy without burning out the adrenal glands. Milky Oats (Avena sativa) is a deep restorative for a nervous system that has been running on overdrive. It nourishes the myelin sheath, the protective coating around the nerves, helping to rebuild the body’s capacity to handle stress. When you take a dropperful of [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20), you are offering your frayed nerves a deep, nourishing drink, a reminder that even in the midst of the fire, the body can find moments of repair.

This is not pathology, this is biology. The grief and the rage are not signs that you are broken; they are signs that you are alive, that you are connected to the earth, that your body is responding exactly as it should to a world in crisis. The work is not to fix these emotions, but to feel them, to track them in the muscles and fascia, and to support the body as it does the heavy lifting of processing the unimaginable. The jaw remembers, the psoas remembers, and with the right support, the body can learn to carry the weight without breaking.
